segments.s588-g41: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past (they went/left); in classical Persian poetry the finite verb often appears before its subject, so the verb precedes the names. نعمان و منذر are the coordinated proper names (subjects). ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from the place/position' (ز = poetic 'from', جای = 'place, position').
segments.s588-g42: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to previously mentioned men; نیزه‌داران is a compound noun 'spear‑bearers' and پاکیزه‌رای is an adjectival epithet following the noun (typical Persian order), a poetic compound meaning 'pure in counsel/mind'.
segments.s588-g43: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. منذر is the subject. ببهرام (ب + بهرام) is the dative/goal 'to Bahram' (written together in poetry). نزدیک شد is a verb phrase 'became near / approached' (شد = copula/auxiliary 'became'), so the clause reads 'when Munḏir approached Bahram.'
segments.s588-g44: ز here is ablative 'from/because of'. گرد in this context means 'dust' (the dust raised by movement). سپه is 'the army/host', so ز گرد سپه = 'from the army's dust'. روز تاریک شد is a result clause 'the day became dark' with شد as the past of 'to become.'
segments.s588-g45: پیاده شدند is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'they became on-foot' i.e. 'they dismounted' (پیاده = 'on foot', شدند = past 'became/they became'). آن دو آزادمرد is the postverbal subject 'those two free/noble men' (آن = demonstrative).
segments.s588-g46: همی is a classical particle often giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept / was continually'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here 3rd person singular form); بهرام is the subject after the verb (verb–subject order is common in poetry), so 'Bahram kept saying'. تیمار و درد are the words he utters — 'tīmār' = 'care' and 'dard' = emotional sorrow/affliction — presented without quotation marks as was normal in classical Persian.
segments.s588-g47: ز گفتار او is an ablative phrase 'from/because of his speech/words'. چند here functions adverbially 'for a while / somewhat' (classical usage), modifying گریست 'he wept'. Thus the clause means 'Munḏir wept for a time because of his words.'
segments.s588-g48: بپرسید is the past verb 'he asked'. گفت here introduces reported speech (literally 'asked, saying:'). اختر شاه چیست is the direct question 'what is the king's star?' — چی (what) + ست (is) with شاه in genitive relation to اختر (the star of the king). The copula is split as چی + ست, a common metrical/poetic arrangement.
segments.s588-g33: Word order is preposition + indirect object + verb + quantifier + noun: به درویش بخشید بسیار چیز = 'he gave to the dervish many things.' بخشید is a past 3rd-person singular verb here meaning 'bestowed/gave' rather than 'forgave.' بسیار modifies چیز as 'many (things)'.
segments.s588-g34: وزان is the fused poetic form of و ز آن (and from that); جایگه is a physical/place noun; رفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to go'; آراست is a past 3rd-person verb here meaning 'prepared/arranged' (took care of, made ready); نیز is an enclitic 'also/too' attaching to the clause. The construction thus means 'and from that place he made ready to go as well.'
segments.s588-g35: همه زیردستان خود را بخواند places the quantifier and object before the verb: 'he summoned all his subordinates.' خود is a possessive/emphatic 'his own,' را is the definite-object marker, and بخواند (classical narrative form) here means 'called/summoned.'
segments.s588-g36: شب تیره is a noun phrase 'dark night' placed before the simile; چون باد = 'like the wind' (چون as a simile marker). لشکر is the object 'the army/host' and براند is an older narrative form of the verb 'to drive' (3rd-person), used here to report the past action 'he drove/led (the host).' The simile modifies how he drove the host: 'he drove the host like a dark wind.'
segments.s588-g37: به یاران marks the indirect object 'to his companions.' همی is an archaic/emphatic particle used with verbs in epic narration (gives a continuous or emphatic flavor to the speech). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list did not include the verbal sense, so see note). یزدان سپاس is an exclamation 'thanks to God' (literally 'God [is] thanks').
segments.s588-g38: که introduces the content clause 'that ...' رفتیم is past first-person plural 'we left/we have gone'; و is 'and'; ایمن شدیم is a light copular/vernacular sequence meaning 'we became safe' or 'we are safe' (ایمن = safe; شدیم = became/are), and از هراس = 'from fear/terror.' Together: 'that we have gone and are safe from fear.'
segments.s588-g39: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = when/as), به نزدیک = 'to/near the vicinity of' (here marking approach), شهر یمن = 'the city of Yemen.' The whole phrase = 'when he came near the city of Yemen.'
segments.s588-g40: پذیره شدش is a semi-passive/periphrastic phrase: پذیره (reception/welcome) + شدش ('befell him' or 'was to him') = 'he was received/welcomed.' The following series کودک و مرد و زن lists the groups who received him: 'children and men and women.' Word order places the reception verb before the list of recipients for poetic effect.
segments.s588-g49: بدو گفت is a typical classical word order meaning “(he) said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت = said). Following that is the wish clause introduced elliptically: بهرام کو خود مباد = “may Bahram himself not be (such).” کو here is a relative/archaic marker linking to the following predicate, خود = ‘himself’, and مباد is the negative optative (‘may it not be’).
segments.s588-g50: که introduces the content of the wish (‘that’). گیرد is a present/subjunctive verb meaning ‘seizes/takes’ (here: may seize). ز = ‘by/from’ (poetic) marks the agent/instrument (his ill star). شوم is the adjective ‘ill‑omened/unlucky’, اخترش = ‘his star’ (اختر + -ش), نیز = ‘also’, and یاد = ‘memory/remembrance’ (here = mind, wit).
segments.s588-g51: که at the start here links/explains the reason or consequence (‘for/that’). هر کو is a classical indefinite relative = ‘whoever’ (هر = any/each; کو functions as the relative marker). نیاید is a negative present form ‘does not come/does not go’ (verb at the end), and به راه خرد = ‘to the path/way of wisdom’ (به + راه + خرد).
segments.s588-g52: ز کردار = ‘because of/on account of deeds’ (ز = because of here; کردار = deeds). ترسم is first‑person present ‘I fear’. که introduces the feared outcome (‘that…’). کیفر = ‘punishment/retribution’, and برد is used in the sense ‘to carry/take/come as consequence’ (here lexical sense not listed in the provided senses), so it is the resultative verb ‘will be borne/taken’.
segments.s588-g53: فرود آوریدش is an imperative phrase meaning ‘bring him down’ or ‘let him alight’ (فرود = down/alighting element; آوریدش = bring him). هم‌انجا = ‘at that very place/there’, که بود = relative clause ‘where he was’ (که + بود, with بود = was). Word order is verb first (imperative) then locative phrase.
segments.s588-g54: بران = ‘thereupon’ / ‘on that’ (an adverbial/relational particle). نیکوی نیکویها is a poetic doubl-ing/intensification meaning roughly ‘the goodness of the good’ or ‘excellence of excellences’. فزود is a past tense verb ‘increased/added’. The clause means that, as a result, that high excellence was increased.
segments.s588-g55: بجز = ‘except’. بزم و میدان lists the only things he had as occupations: بزم = ‘feast/banquet/revelry’ and میدان = ‘field/arena’ (here: battlefield or martial field). نبودیش is a nominalized/poetic expression meaning ‘there was not of his’ / ‘he lacked’ (i.e., he had no other work). کار = ‘work/occupation’.
segments.s588-g56: وگر = ‘but if’ / ‘otherwise’ (marks the alternative reading). بخشش = ‘giving, generosity’ (here the sense is donation/gift). و کوشش = ‘and effort/exertion’. کارزار = ‘battle/war’ (here ‘the business of war’). The line says that apart from feasts and the field, his acts of giving and his exertions were the work of battle.
segments.s588-g1: چنان بد که is a result clause: چنان (so) + بد (bad/ill) + که (that) introduce the consequence. یک روز در بزمگاه is a temporal/place phrase 'one day in the banquet-hall' that attaches to the clause (word order: temporal phrase follows the subordinating که-clause).
segments.s588-g2: همی بود is an older continuous past: همی (still/continually) + بود (was). بر پای در is a fixed locative image 'on the foot of the door' = 'at the threshold/doorway', where در is the noun 'door'. پیش شاه 'in the presence of the king' follows as the goal/location phrase. The verb precedes the locatives in this poetic order.
segments.s588-g3: چو شد تیره is a temporal clause: چو (when) + شد (became) + تیره (dark). بر پای خواب آمدش is an idiomatic expression: بر پای (at the feet/while standing) + خواب آمدش (sleep came upon him) — the verb آمدش carries the enclitic -ش 'to him'.
segments.s588-g4: هم از ایستادن شتاب آمدش: هم (also) introduces a parallel effect; از ایستادن is a cause/source 'from standing'; شتاب آمدش literally 'haste/impetus came to him' — i.e. he became hurried or restless. Again آمدش is 'came to him' with the enclitic -ش.
segments.s588-g5: پدر چون بدیدش is a temporal clause 'when the father saw him' (چون = since/when, بدیدش = 'he saw him', -ش = him). بهم برده چشم is a participial description: بهم برده (taken together/drawn together) modifies چشم (the eyes) — i.e. 'his eyes drew/narrowed together.' The past participle precedes the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s588-g6: به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with sharpness/violence' (به + تندی), یکی بانگ 'a single cry' (یکی = one/a single, بانگ = cry/sound). برزد is an archaic/perhaps poetic verb meaning 'struck out/uttered' (placed after the object), and به خشم 'in anger' specifies the manner.
segments.s588-g7: به دژخیم فرمود is 'he said/commanded to the torturer' (به = to, دژخیم = torturer/strong man, فرمود = he commanded/said). کو را ببر is an imperative phrase: کو here functions as 'that one/which one' (object), را is the definite object marker, and ببر is the imperative 'take!' — i.e. 'Take him away.'
segments.s588-g8: کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (کزین = from this, پس = after/then). نبیند is a negative-subjunctive/potential form 'he shall not see' (here expressing future prohibition/result). کلاه و کمر 'hat and belt' are the objects — symbolic of a man's honor/status which he is henceforth to be denied.
segments.s588-g17: This is a coordinated noun phrase (object) listing what was sent. The items are joined by و (and). باژ روم is a genitive/attributive phrase in which باژ (noise/cry) is qualified by روم (of Rome) in an older, compact word order.
segments.s588-g18: فرستاد is the simple past 'sent' (verb) and the subject (قیصر) follows it in this poetic inverted order; به آباد بوم is a prepositional phrase 'to the prosperous land' where آباد qualifies بوم.
segments.s588-g19: چو آمد = 'when (he) came' (چو as temporal conjunction); شهنشاه is the subject 'the king/emperor'; بنواختش is a past verb with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'), here 'he struck him' (بنواخت- + -ش).
segments.s588-g20: سزاوار (appropriate/fitting) governs جایگه ('place, here: rank/position'); او is the beneficiary 'for him'; ساختش is past 'made/built' with enclitic -ش again referring back to the beneficiary or the thing made — overall: 'he made for him a fitting position.'
segments.s588-g21: فرستاد (past 'sent') is the main verb, بهرام is the subject. زی او is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'to him' (here marking the indirect object), and پیام is the direct object 'message/tidings.'
segments.s588-g22: This is the content of the message. که‌ای combines a literary particle + vocative (roughly 'O' or 'that: O'); مرد is the vocative 'man'; بیدار is an imperative/adjectival 'awake' or 'be alert'; گسترده کام is an adjectival phrase 'wide-spread in desire' (گسترده modifying کام = 'desire/appetite').
segments.s588-g23: ز = 'from/by' (poetic), کهتر = 'one inferior' or 'someone lesser', به چیزی = 'by/with anything' (literally 'to/for something'), and بیازرد here expresses the notion 'be harmed/annoyed' in a negative or dubitative sense (rhetorical: 'that the king should be harmed by anything from an inferior?'). شاه at the end names the one affected ('the king').
segments.s588-g24: ازو ('from him') + دور گشتم ('I became distant' — دور adjective + گشتم past 'I became/turned') describes the speaker's withdrawal; چنین بی‌گناه = 'thus innocent/blameless' states the speaker's claim of innocence. Word order places the prepositional phrase first (ازو) for emphasis.
segments.s588-g9: بدو here is a dative-style clitic meaning 'to him / onto him' (equivalent to به او). خانه زندان کن is an imperative clause: noun phrase 'خانه زندان' ('prison-house') followed by the imperative کن ('do, make, put'), i.e. 'put him into the prison-house.' و بازگرد is a coordination: و 'and' plus the imperative بازگرد 'return.'
segments.s588-g10: نزیبد is an impersonal evaluative 'it does not befit / it is not fitting.' It governs the following verbal notion; برو here functions as the verbal element meaning 'to go' (present/subjunctive/optative sense after نزیبد). گاه و ننگ و نبرد is a coordinated list of noun-objects ('occasion/assembly,' 'shame/disgrace,' 'battle') — the things it is said not to befit him to attend.
segments.s588-g11: به ایوان introduces a locative 'at/at the portico.' همی بود is the older continuous/past usage 'he was still / continued to be.' خسته جگر is a compact adjectival phrase with جگر used figuratively 'heart/inner feeling' and خسته 'weary' — 'his heart was weary.'
segments.s588-g12: ندید is the past negative 'did not see.' اندران سال is an archaic locative expression meaning 'in that year' or 'during that year' (اندر + آن). روی پدر is literal 'the face of the father' (روی = face; پدر = father), i.e. 'he did not see his father's face that year.'
segments.s588-g13: مگر is used here as 'except for' (introducing exceptions). The following nouns form an enumerated exception: مهر (the festival Mehregān / a proper-name festival), نوروز (Nowruz), and جشن سده (the Sadeh festival).
segments.s588-g14: که introduces the subordinate/relative clause 'when/that.' او is the subject 'he.' پیش رفتی is a compound past sense 'went forward / advanced' (پیش = forward/before + رفتی, archaic past form of رفتن), and میان رده means 'among the ranks/rows' (میان = among, رده = row/rank).
segments.s588-g15: چنان بد که is a common correlative: 'so ... that ...' (چنان = so/such; بد = bad/ill) introducing the result clause. طینوش رومی functions as a name plus epithet 'Tinush the Roman' (طینوش treated as a proper name here). ز راه is the poetic preposition 'from/by the road/way.'
segments.s588-g16: فرستاده آمد is a simple past narrative: فرستاده 'a messenger / the sent one' as noun and آمد 'came.' به نزدیک شاه is a locative/directional phrase 'to the nearness of the king' = 'came up to the king / came into the king's presence.'
segments.s588-g25: تو is the emphatic/subject pronoun 'you'. خواهش is the noun 'a plea/request' used with the following subjunctive. کنی‌گر is an archaic conditional made of the subjunctive کنی + the particle گر = 'if you do/if you were to', ت here is the 2nd-person object enclitic 'you' (appearing separated in the line) and را is the definite/object marker; بخشدم is past tense 'I forgave' (verb-final idea: 'If you had pleaded, I forgave you').
segments.s588-g26: مگر here functions as an adversative conjunction 'but/however' (appearing before the following clause). بخت is the personified 'fortune/fate', پژمرده is its adjective 'withered/faded', and بدرخشدم (past) literally 'I shone' — a poetic way to say 'my (formerly) faded fortune began to shine'.
segments.s588-g27: سوی is the preposition 'toward/to'. دایگانم = دایگان + -م 'my wet‑nurse' (possessive suffix). فرستد is the classical past/epic form of 'to send' (here 'sent'). مگر begins the clause in the fixed pairing مگر که = 'except that/namely', introducing the explanatory subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s588-g28: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause. منذر is the proper name Munḏir (the subject of the clause). The element labeled م functions here as a terse classical linking/copular element (rendered in translation by 'was' or understood in the clause), and را marks/emphases the object/personal target in older word order (forming effectively مرا 'to/for me' in context). به ز is the comparative construction 'better than', and مام و پدر = 'mother and father'. The whole clause thus says Munḏir was to me better than mother and father.
segments.s588-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. طینوش is a personal name (subject). بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. پیغام اوی = 'his message' (پیغام 'message' followed by the possessive اوی 'his' placed after the noun in this older/poetic order).
segments.s588-g30: برآورد is the past of برآوردن 'to accomplish/bring about' = 'he fulfilled/accomplished'. ازان is the adverbial 'from that/thereupon' referring back to the message. آرزو کام is a poetic collocation meaning 'fulfilment of desire' (آرزو 'wish' + کام 'satisfaction/fulfilment'), and اوی at the end is the possessive 'his' — so 'he accomplished/fulfilled his desire as a result of that.'
segments.s588-g31: دل‌آزار is an epithet 'heart‑troubling/heart‑afflicting' placed before the name to modify it; بهرام is the proper name. زان = 'from that/therefore' (referring to the prior event). شاد is the adjective 'happy/glad' and گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became' (i.e. 'became glad').
segments.s588-g32: وزان = و + زان 'and from that/and so'. بند is 'bond/fetter' (the thing constraining someone). بی‌مایه is the adjective 'worthless' modifying بند. آزاد گشت is the predication 'became free/was freed' (آزاد adjective + گشت 'became') — together 'that worthless bond became free/was loosed.'

sentences.s588-s21: Nuʿmān and Munḏir left their place — those same spear-bearers, pure in counsel.
sentences.s588-s22: When Munḏir drew near to Bahram, the day grew dark from the army's dust.
sentences.s588-s23: Those two noble men dismounted. Bahram kept saying, 'care and sorrow.'
sentences.s588-s24: From his words Munḏir wept for a while; he asked, saying, 'What is the king's star?'
sentences.s588-s17: He gave the dervish many things, and from that place he too made ready to depart.
sentences.s588-s18: He summoned all his subordinates, and like a dark wind he drove the host.
sentences.s588-s19: He said to his companions, 'Thanks be to God; we have gone and become safe from fear.'
sentences.s588-s20: When he came near the city of Yemen, children, men, and women welcomed him.
sentences.s588-s25: He said to him, “May Bahram never be one whose unlucky star also seizes his memory.”
sentences.s588-s26: For whoever does not come to the path of wisdom, I fear that punishment will be the result of his deeds.
sentences.s588-s27: Bring him down right there where he was; thereupon he increased that excellence of excellences.
sentences.s588-s28: He had no occupation except revelry and the battlefield; otherwise his giving and his exertion were the work of war.
sentences.s588-s1: He was so ill that one day in the banquet-hall he stood at the doorway before the king.
sentences.s588-s2: When it grew dark, sleep came upon his feet, and likewise from standing he felt a sudden haste/restlessness.
sentences.s588-s3: When the father saw him, his eyes drew together; with sudden fierceness he uttered a single cry in anger.
sentences.s588-s4: He ordered the executioner, 'Take him away, that from now on he shall not see hat or belt.'
sentences.s588-s9: Caesar sent Aba, Badreh and Bardeh, and the cry of Rome to the prosperous land.
sentences.s588-s10: When the king came, he struck him and made for him a fitting place (a suitable rank).
sentences.s588-s11: Bahram sent him this message: 'O man, awake — your desire is widespread.'
sentences.s588-s12: That the king be harmed by anything from an inferior? — I turned away from him, thus innocent.
sentences.s588-s5: Put him in the prison-house and return; it does not befit him to go to occasions, to shame, or to battle.
sentences.s588-s6: At the portico he still sat, his heart weary; in that year he did not see his father's face.
sentences.s588-s7: Except for Mehregān, Nowruz and the Sadeh festival — when he went forward among the ranks.
sentences.s588-s8: So bad (was he) that Tinush the Roman, sent from the road, came near the king.
sentences.s588-s13: If you had begged, I would have forgiven you; but my withered fortune shone.
sentences.s588-s14: He sent (someone) to my wet-nurse — Munḏir — who was to me better than mother and father.
sentences.s588-s15: When Tinush heard his message, he fulfilled his desire because of it.
sentences.s588-s16: That heart‑troubled Bahram became glad because of it, and that worthless bond was set free.
